--- SOURCE 1: Crispr and plant pathology: Revolutionizing disease ... ---
URL: https://wjarr.com/sites/default/files/WJARR-2024-2996.pdf

SUMMARY:
These case studies illustrate how CRISPR technology can be harnessed to develop fungal-resistant crop varieties, offering sustainable solutions

FULL CONTENT:
 Corresponding author: Damaris Ajayi Copyright © 2024 Author(s) retain the copyright of this article. This article is published under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Liscense 4.0. Crispr and plant pathology: Revolutionizing disease resistance in crops Damaris Ajayi 1, * Olalekan Amoo 2 and Gift Maureen Obunukwu 3 1 Plant Biotechnology, Taskrabbit, University of Agriculture, Krakow, Poland. 2 School of Agriculture and Food Sustainability, The University of Queensland, Brisbane, Australia. 3 Department of Agricultural Science, Tennessee State University, USA. World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 044–060 Publication history: Received on 18 August 2024; revised on 28 September 2024; accepted on 30 September 2024 Article DOI: https://doi.org/10.30574/wjarr.2024.24.1.2996 Abstract CRISPR (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats) technology has revolutionized plant pathology by providing a precise and efficient tool for enhancing disease resistance in crops. This gene-editing technique enables scientists to modify specific DNA sequences in plants, allowing for the targeted improvement of traits related to pathogen defense. By altering genes responsible for susceptibility to diseases, CRISPR facilitates the development of crops that are more resistant to bacterial, fungal, and viral pathogens, thereby reducing the reliance on chemical pesticides. Additionally, CRISPR can be used to boost plant immunity and improve resilience against emerging plant diseases that threaten global food security. The technology's accuracy and speed have accelerated breeding programs, enabling researchers to respond rapidly to agricultural challenges posed by climate change and the evolution of new pathogens. This article explores the breakthroughs achieved through CRISPR in plant pathology, highlighting case studies of its application in major crops such as rice, wheat, and tomatoes. Furthermore, it discusses the ethical, regulatory, and environmental considerations of using CRISPR for crop improvement, alongside its potential to revolutionize sustainable agriculture by reducing disease-induced yield losses and promoting food security. Keywords: CRISPR; Plant Pathology; Disease Resistance; Gene Editing; Sustainable Agriculture 1. Introduction 1.1. Overview of Crop Diseases and Global Food Security Crop diseases are a significant threat to global food security, causing considerable economic losses and reducing agricultural productivity. Pathogens such as fungi, bacteria, viruses, and nematodes infect a wide range of staple crops, including wheat, rice, maize, and potatoes, often leading to severe yield losses and sometimes even total crop failure. For instance, rice blast, caused by Magnaporthe oryzae, destroys enough rice annually to feed over 60 million people (Nalley et al., 2016). Similarly, wheat rust and potato late blight are well-documented for their devastating impacts on agricultural production (Savary et al., 2019). The economic impact of crop diseases extends beyond just food losses. In regions highly dependent on agriculture, such as developing countries, these diseases can lead to catastrophic consequences for smallholder farmers, who typically lack access to advanced disease management tools. Crop disease outbreaks can elevate food prices, disrupt trade, and cause food shortages, further exacerbating issues like hunger and malnutrition. According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), pests and diseases are responsible for 20-40% of global crop production losses annually, with economic losses reaching billions of dollars (FAO, 2021). World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 044–060 45 Figure 1 Plant Diseases and Pathogens Given that the global population is expected to reach 9.7 billion by 2050, addressing crop diseases is essential to ensuring sufficient food production to meet this demand (United Nations, 2019). Conventional methods like chemical pesticides, crop rotation, and breeding for disease resistance have limited success and can sometimes be environmentally harmful. Therefore, innovative approaches such as genetic engineering, specifically CRISPR technology, offer new opportunities to enhance crop disease resistance and improve global food security in a more sustainable manner (Chen et al., 2019). 1.2. Traditional Methods of Disease Resistance in Crops Conventional methods for managing crop diseases primarily involve breeding for resistance and the application of chemical pesticides. Breeding resistant crop varieties has long been a cornerstone of agricultural disease management. By selecting and crossbreeding plants that naturally exhibit resistance to specific pathogens, farmers can cultivate crops with enhanced ability to withstand infections. While this method has been somewhat successful, it is a time-consuming and labour-intensive process that may take several years or even decades to produce results (Singh et al., 2016). Additionally, the rapid evolution of pathogens can render resistant varieties ineffective, as diseases may develop new strains that overcome these defenses. Chemical pesticides, another traditional method, are widely used to control crop diseases by killing or inhibiting the growth of pathogens. While effective in the short term, over-reliance on pesticides poses several issues. Repeated pesticide applications can lead to the development of pesticide-resistant pathogens, diminishing the efficacy of treatments over time (Hawkins et al., 2019). Furthermore, excessive pesticide use has serious environmental consequences, including soil degradation, water contamination, and harm to beneficial insects and biodiversity. Both traditional breeding and pesticide use have limitations in providing long-term, sustainable disease resistance. Breeding often cannot keep pace with the rapid adaptation of pathogens, and pesticides create environmental concerns and can lose effectiveness over time. These limitations underscore the need for innovative approaches, such as genome editing tools like CRISPR, which offer more precise and durable solutions for enhancing disease resistance in crops. 1.3. Introduction to CRISPR and Its Role in Plant Pathology CRISPR (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats) is a revolutionary gene-editing technology that has transformed the field of biology, including plant pathology. Initially discovered as part of the bacterial immune system, CRISPR has been adapted to precisely modify DNA in a wide variety of organisms, including plants. The World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 044–060 46 precision and efficiency of CRISPR-Cas9, the most widely used variant, make it a game-changer for improving disease resistance in crops. CRISPR works by using a guide RNA to target a specific DNA sequence in the genome, where the Cas9 protein makes a cut. Once the DNA is cut, natural repair mechanisms are triggered, allowing scientists to either disable genes or insert new genetic material. This precision allows for highly targeted modifications, meaning that specific genes associated with disease susceptibility can be edited to enhance resistance (Zhang et al., 2019). In plant pathology, CRISPR provides the ability to directly modify genes responsible for a plant’s immune response to pathogens, leading to the development of crops with enhanced resistance to bacteria, fungi, and viruses. Unlike traditional breeding methods, which are time-consuming and limited by the plant’s natural gene pool, CRISPR allows for rapid and precise edits that can overcome the evolving nature of pathogens (Jaganathan et al., 2018). Moreover, CRISPR technology minimizes the need for chemical pesticides, making it a more environmentally sustainable option. As a result, CRISPR offers immense potential in improving crop health and contributing to global food security through better disease management. 2. Understanding CRISPR technology 2.1. The CRISPR-Cas9 System: Mechanism and Function The CRISPR-Cas9 system is a groundbreaking gene-editing tool that allows scientists to make precise and targeted modifications to an organism's DNA. Originally discovered as a bacterial defense mechanism against viruses, CRISPR has since been adapted for use in various organisms, including plants. The system's precision, efficiency, and flexibility make it one of the most powerful tools in genetic engineering. At its core, CRISPR-Cas9 functions by using two key components: the guide RNA (gRNA) and the Cas9 enzyme. The gRNA is designed to be complementary to a specific DNA sequence within the organism's genome. This ensures that the CRISPR system targets the desired location for editing. Once the gRNA binds to the target DNA, the Cas9 enzyme acts like molecular scissors, making a double-strand cut at the specified site (Doudna & Charpentier, 2014). After the DNA is cut, the cell’s natural repair mechanisms are triggered. The two main repair pathways are non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) and homology-directed repair (HDR). NHEJ often results in small insertions or deletions, which can disrupt gene function, effectively "knocking out" the targeted gene. On the other hand, HDR allows for more precise editing by using a DNA template to repair the break, enabling the introduction of specific genetic sequences (Jinek et al., 2012). Figure 2 CRISPR-Cas9 System One of the key strengths of CRISPR-Cas9 is its flexibility. The guide RNA can be easily customized to target almost any gene, providing a high degree of control over where the DNA is cut. This precision is crucial in plant pathology, where it enables scientists to target specific genes related to disease resistance, stress tolerance, or crop yield improvement. Furthermore, CRISPR can target multiple genes simultaneously, a process known as multiplexing, which is particularly useful in complex plant genomes where traits are controlled by several genes (Cong et al., 2013). Another advantage of World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 044–060 47 CRISPR-Cas9 is its efficiency. Traditional gene-editing methods, such as zinc finger nucleases (ZFNs) and transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs), require complex protein engineering for each target sequence, making them less flexible and more time-consuming. In contrast, CRISPR only requires designing a new guide RNA for each new target, significantly reducing the time and effort needed for gene modification (Gaj et al., 2013). The precision and adaptability of CRISPR-Cas9 have revolutionized genetic engineering, offering unprecedented opportunities for improving crops. By enabling the precise editing of genes associated with disease susceptibility, stress response, and growth, CRISPR can contribute to the development of crops that are more resilient, productive, and environmentally sustainable. 2.2. CRISPR in Plants: The Basics CRISPR technology has revolutionized plant genetics by providing a highly precise and efficient method for editing plant genomes. Its application in plants involves various strategies, including gene knockouts, knock-ins, and targeted mutations, all of which can be used to improve disease resistance, enhance growth, and increase yield. In plants, CRISPR is used to target specific genes that play a role in susceptibility to diseases. Gene knockouts, one of the most common CRISPR applications, involve disabling or "knocking out" genes that make plants vulnerable to pathogens. By inactivating these genes, plants can become resistant to diseases without the need for external chemical inputs like pesticides. For instance, researchers have successfully knocked out genes that make tomatoes and wheat susceptible to fungal infections, significantly enhancing their resistance (Wang et al., 2014). Another approach is gene knock-ins, where CRISPR is used to insert beneficial genes into the plant genome. This method allows for the introduction of new traits, such as disease resistance genes from other plant species. These knock-ins can make plants more resilient to a variety of pathogens, including bacteria, fungi, and viruses (Borrelli et al., 2018). CRISPR can also introduce precise mutations to enhance plant resistance. By altering specific DNA sequences, researchers can improve a plant’s ability to recognize and fight off pathogens. For example, mutations in certain receptor genes can enhance the plant’s immune response, leading to increased resistance against a range of diseases (Zaidi et al., 2020). Overall, CRISPR’s ability to create targeted, stable, and heritable mutations makes it a powerful tool for improving plant health and resilience. It enables more sustainable crop management by reducing the reliance on chemical treatments and increasing long-term disease resistance. 2.3. Advantages of CRISPR over Traditional Genetic Modification CRISPR technology offers several significant advantages over traditional genetic modification (GM) methods and conventional breeding techniques, particularly in terms of speed, precision, and efficiency. Traditional GMOs involve inserting foreign genes into a plant’s genome, often through time-consuming processes that rely on random integration of genes. CRISPR, on the other hand, allows for highly targeted modifications by precisely editing the plant’s existing DNA. This targeted approach drastically reduces the time required for genetic improvements, often cutting years off the development process (Chen et al., 2019). One of the main benefits of CRISPR is its precision. The technology allows scientists to pinpoint and modify specific genes with minimal off-target effects. This is in contrast to earlier genetic engineering techniques, which often resulted in unintended changes in other parts of the genome, potentially leading to unpredictable traits. The ability to avoid off-target mutations makes CRISPR a safer and more reliable tool for plant breeding (Zhang et al., 2019). Additionally, CRISPR is more efficient than conventional breeding, which relies on crossing plants over multiple generations to achieve desired traits. This process can take years and may still be limited by the plant’s genetic diversity. CRISPR bypasses these limitations, enabling faster development of crops with enhanced disease resistance and other beneficial traits. 3. Key plant pathogens and their economic impact 3.1. Fungal Pathogens Fungal pathogens are among the most destructive forces affecting global agriculture, significantly reducing crop yields and threatening food security. These pathogens are responsible for a wide range of diseases, including rusts, smuts, and blights, which collectively impact some of the world’s most essential crops, such as wheat, rice, and corn. Rusts, caused by fungal species in the Puccinia genus, are one of the most damaging fungal diseases, particularly affecting wheat. Wheat rust, especially stem rust (Puccinia graminis), can lead to yield losses of up to 70%, and in severe cases, total crop failure. Rust spores are easily spread by the wind, allowing infections to spread quickly over large areas, making it difficult to control. One notable outbreak of wheat rust, known as the Ug99 strain, first identified in Uganda, World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 044–060 48 threatens wheat production across Africa and Asia and has prompted international concern due to its ability to overcome resistant wheat varieties (Singh et al., 2015). Smuts, another category of fungal diseases, affect grains like corn and wheat. Corn smut, caused by Ustilago maydis, leads to the formation of tumor-like galls on corn ears, kernels, and leaves, significantly reducing crop quality and yield. In some cases, these smuts can cause total losses, particularly in developing countries where farmers rely on corn as a staple food crop. Similarly, wheat smut, caused by species like Tilletia caries, can contaminate grains, rendering them unsellable and posing health risks if consumed. Blights are another fungal threat, with rice blast (Magnaporthe oryzae) being a prime example. Rice blast affects rice, one of the most critical staple crops globally, leading to yield losses of 30-50% in affected regions. The disease damages leaves, stems, and grains, reducing photosynthesis and ultimately causing the plant to die prematurely. Rice blast is particularly prevalent in tropical and subtropical climates, where high humidity provides ideal conditions for fungal growth (Nalley et al., 2016). The economic impact of fungal pathogens is enormous. Global losses due to wheat rust alone are estimated to be in the billions of dollars annually, and the total losses from fungal diseases on all crops exceed 125 billion dollars per year (Fisher et al., 2012). Traditional methods of combating fungal pathogens, such as fungicide application, crop rotation, and the development of resistant varieties, are often inadequate due to the rapid evolution of fungal strains. Fungal pathogens adapt quickly, overcoming both chemical treatments and genetic resistance in crops, presenting a continuing challenge to agricultural productivity. Innovative solutions, such as CRISPR-based genetic modifications, are increasingly seen as necessary to provide more sustainable and durable forms of fungal disease resistance. 3.2. Bacterial Pathogens Bacterial pathogens are another major cause of crop diseases, significantly affecting the yields and quality of various crops. Two of the most prevalent and destructive bacterial diseases are bacterial blight and bacterial wilt, which primarily affect crops like tomatoes, potatoes, and rice. Bacterial blight is caused by different species of the Xanthomonas genus and is particularly problematic in rice. Rice bacterial blight (Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae) is one of the most severe bacterial diseases affecting rice production worldwide. It causes lesions on leaves, leading to reduced photosynthesis, stunted growth, and decreased grain production. In regions like Southeast Asia, where rice is a staple food crop, bacterial blight can lead to yield losses of 20-30%, with some outbreaks resulting in complete crop failure. The disease spreads easily through water and wind, especially in warm, humid climates, making it difficult to control (Nino-Liu et al., 2006). Bacterial wilt, caused by Ralstonia solanacearum, affects a wide range of crops, including tomatoes, potatoes, and bananas. The pathogen invades the plant's vascular system, causing wilting, yellowing of leaves, and eventually plant death. In tomatoes and potatoes, bacterial wilt can devastate fields, causing significant economic losses. For example, in potato production, Ralstonia solanacearum can result in total crop failure in heavily infected areas. The pathogen persists in soil and water, making it challenging to eradicate, and it spreads rapidly in warm, moist conditions (Mansfield et al., 2012). The economic impact of bacterial pathogens is substantial. For example, bacterial wilt alone causes over $1 billion in losses annually in crops like potatoes and tomatoes (Elphinstone, 2005). Traditional control methods, including crop rotation, resistant varieties, and chemical treatments, have had limited success due to the bacteria's ability to survive in soil and water for long periods. This highlights the need for more sustainable solutions, such as CRISPR-based genetic modifications, to develop crops with enhanced resistance to bacterial pathogens. 3.3. Viral Pathogens Viral pathogens are a significant threat to global agriculture, capable of causing severe yield losses and economic damage. Viral diseases such as the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) and the tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV) are especially devastating, affecting a wide range of crops, including tobacco, tomatoes, and cucumbers. Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) is one of the most well-known and widespread plant viruses. It infects tobacco, tomatoes, peppers, and other members of the Solanaceae family. TMV causes mosaic-like patterns on leaves, stunted growth, and reduced yields. In tobacco, it can cause crop losses of up to 30%, while in tomatoes, yield reductions can be even more severe. TMV is highly resilient and can persist in soil and on surfaces for long periods, making it difficult to control World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 044–060 49 through conventional methods (Agrios, 2005). The virus is easily transmitted through mechanical damage, such as when handling plants or contaminated tools, further complicating management. Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV), another destructive viral pathogen, primarily affects tomatoes and cucumbers. TYLCV is transmitted by the whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) and causes severe stunting, leaf curling, yellowing, and reduced fruit production. Infected plants often yield little to no fruit, leading to devastating economic losses. The virus has spread globally, particularly in warm, tropical, and subtropical regions, where whitefly populations thrive. In the Mediterranean region, TYLCV has caused tomato yield losses of up to 90% in some outbreaks (Hanssen et al., 2010). Cucumber mosaic virus (CMV) is another critical pathogen, infecting over 1,200 plant species, including cucumbers, tomatoes, and peppers. CMV causes leaf distortion, yellowing, and mosaic patterns, leading to significant yield reductions. Like TYLCV, CMV is spread by insect vectors such as aphids, and once a plant is infected, there is no effective cure (Zitter et al., 1996). The economic impact of viral diseases is considerable, as they are difficult to control once established. Conventional methods like pesticide use to manage insect vectors and planting virus-resistant varieties have shown limited success. As viral pathogens continue to evolve, innovative genetic approaches such as CRISPR offer promising solutions for developing crops with durable resistance. 3.4. Nematode and Insect Pest-Related Diseases Nematodes and insect pests play a crucial role in the spread of plant diseases, significantly impacting agricultural productivity and economic stability. Nematodes, particularly root-knot nematodes (Meloidogyne spp.), invade plant root systems, causing galls and disrupting nutrient and water uptake. These nematodes weaken plants, making them more susceptible to secondary infections from fungi and bacteria, resulting in yield losses that can exceed 50% in crops such as tomatoes and potatoes (Jones et al., 2013). Their presence can also limit crop rotation options and decrease soil health, leading to further declines in productivity. Insects, particularly those that act as vectors, are equally damaging. Aphids, whiteflies, and thrips can transmit various viral and bacterial pathogens while feeding on plant sap. For example, aphids are known vectors of the cucumber mosaic virus and the potato virus Y, leading to severe crop losses. Insect infestations can weaken plants, induce stress, and create entry points for pathogens, compounding the effects of viral diseases (Grafton-Cardwell et al., 2014). The combined effects of nematodes and insect pests contribute to significant economic losses in agriculture, necessitating integrated pest management strategies that address both the pests and the diseases they facilitate. 4. CRISPR and disease resistance in crops 4.1. Editing Disease Resistance Genes with CRISPR CRISPR technology has emerged as a transformative tool in plant breeding, particularly for enhancing disease resistance in crops. By allowing precise modifications of specific genes associated with plant immunity, CRISPR enables scientists to develop varieties that are better equipped to combat various pathogens, including fungi, bacteria, and viruses. At the core of CRISPR's effectiveness in editing disease resistance genes is the CRISPR-Cas9 system, which employs a guide RNA (gRNA) to direct the Cas9 nuclease to specific DNA sequences within the plant genome. When the Cas9 protein reaches its target, it introduces a double-strand break in the DNA. The plant’s natural repair mechanisms then kick in, allowing for two primary approaches to gene editing: non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) and homology-directed repair (HDR). Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ) is the most common pathway employed in CRISPR editing. When the double-strand break occurs, the repair process can lead to insertions or deletions (indels) at the target site, effectively disrupting the gene function. This method is particularly useful for knocking out genes that negatively regulate disease resistance or are involved in susceptibility pathways. For instance, researchers have successfully knocked out specific susceptibility genes in rice, resulting in enhanced resistance to bacterial blight by disabling the pathways that the bacteria exploit to infect the plant (Liu et al., 2018). Homology-Directed Repair (HDR) is a more precise method that allows the introduction of specific mutations or the addition of beneficial genes. By providing a DNA template alongside the CRISPR machinery, scientists can guide the plant to incorporate desired traits, such as resistance genes from wild relatives or other species. This method has been used in various crops, including wheat, to introduce resistance genes against fungal pathogens like wheat rust (Wang et World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 044–060 50 al., 2014). Furthermore, CRISPR can also be used to enhance the natural disease resistance mechanisms of plants, such as the hypersensitive response and systemic acquired resistance. By editing genes involved in these pathways, crops can be made more resilient to a broader range of pathogens. For instance, scientists have targeted genes responsible for the production of phytoalexins—antimicrobial compounds produced by plants—in tomato, leading to improved resistance against various pathogens (Gao et al., 2016). In summary, CRISPR technology provides a robust platform for editing disease resistance genes in crops, enabling precise modifications that enhance the plants' ability to fend off infections. By targeting specific genes and pathways, researchers can create crop varieties that not only withstand current threats but are also better prepared for future challenges posed by evolving pathogens. 4.2. Case Study: CRISPR for Fungal Resistance A notable example of CRISPR's application in developing fungal-resistant crops can be seen in recent advancements in rice and wheat, targeting specific genes associated with resistance to fungal pathogens. In rice, researchers have successfully used CRISPR-Cas9 to enhance resistance against the notorious fungal pathogen Magnaporthe oryzae, which causes rice blast disease. This disease is one of the most significant threats to global rice production, leading to yield losses of up to 50% in affected areas. A study conducted by Liu et al. (2018) demonstrated the potential of CRISPR to knock out the susceptibility gene OsERF922, which is implicated in the plant's susceptibility to the pathogen. By disrupting this gene, the researchers created a rice variety that exhibited enhanced resistance to rice blast without compromising yield or other agronomic traits. The resulting CRISPR-edited rice showed a marked reduction in disease symptoms and a significant improvement in overall plant health, showcasing the effectiveness of gene editing in enhancing disease resistance. Similarly, in wheat, researchers have targeted the Fusarium species, responsible for Fusarium head blight (FHB), which devastates wheat crops globally. In a study by Wang et al. (2019), scientists employed CRISPR-Cas9 to edit the wheat genome, focusing on genes that regulate the plant’s defense mechanisms against Fusarium graminearum. By knocking out specific susceptibility genes, they developed wheat strains with significantly improved resistance to FHB. The edited plants not only exhibited reduced fungal infection rates but also demonstrated enhanced resilience in field conditions, proving the potential of CRISPR technology in combating this destructive fungal disease. These case studies illustrate how CRISPR technology can be harnessed to develop fungal-resistant crop varieties, offering sustainable solutions to some of agriculture's most pressing challenges. 4.3. Case Study: CRISPR for Bacterial Resistance CRISPR technology has made significant strides in developing bacterial-resistant crops, particularly in potatoes and tomatoes, which are often affected by various bacterial pathogens. One prominent case study involves the use of CRISPR to enhance resistance to bacterial blight in tomatoes, primarily caused by Pseudomonas syringae. In a groundbreaking study conducted by Zhang et al. (2020), researchers targeted the SlPto gene in tomatoes, which encodes a receptor that recognizes specific bacterial effectors. By utilizing CRISPR-Cas9, the scientists successfully edited this gene, resulting in tomato plants with improved resistance to bacterial blight. The modified plants exhibited reduced disease symptoms and maintained higher fruit yields compared to their unedited counterparts. This study not only demonstrated the potential of CRISPR to create disease-resistant tomato varieties but also highlighted the efficiency and precision of gene editing in agricultural applications. Similarly, in potatoes, researchers have targeted Ralstonia solanacearum, the causal agent of bacterial wilt, which leads to significant economic losses in potato production. A team led by Gao et al. (2019) utilized CRISPR to knock out susceptibility genes in potato plants, enhancing their resistance to bacterial wilt. The edited potatoes showed a remarkable decrease in disease severity and improved survival rates under conditions of high pathogen pressure. The success of this approach underscores the effectiveness of CRISPR technology in combating bacter

This article is part of the Research TopicCharacterizing and Improving Traits for Resilient Crop Development[View all 15 articles](https://www.frontiersin.org/research-topics/46684/characterizing-and-improving-traits-for-resilient-crop-development/magazine)

# CRISPR/Cas9 based *mlo*-mediated resistance against *Podosphaera xanthii* in cucumber (*Cucumis sativus* L.)

![Mumin Ibrahim Tek](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1691365/74)
![Ozer Calis*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1936675/74)
![Hakan Fidan](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)
![Mehraj D. Shah*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2113553/74)
![Sefanur Celik](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)
![Shabir Hussain Wani,*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/59758/74)

Powdery mildews (PM) are common and severe pathogen groups that threaten plants, and PM resistance is complex and polygenic in cucumbers. Previously *mlo*-based resistance was reported in various plants, including cucumber, with generated loss-of *CsaMLO* function mutants. However, *mlo*-based resistance in cucumber is also complex and involves additional mechanisms such as hypersensitive response (HR) and papillae formation. For this reason, we focused on determining the *mlo*-based powdery mildew resistance mechanism in cucumber. CRISPR/Cas9 was used in the present study to generate loss-of-function mutants for *CsaMLO1*, *CsaMLO8*, and *CsaMLO11* of PM susceptible ADR27 cucumber inbred lines and *CsaMLO* mutants were obtained and validated. Trypan Blue and DAB staining were performed to detect *Podosphaera xanthii* germination/penetration rates and accumulation of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS). Our results indicate that PM-susceptibility associated *CsaMLO*s in cucumber are negative regulators in different defense mechanisms against powdery mildew at early and late stages of infection. Further, the experiment results indicated that *CsaMLO8* mutation-based resistance was associated with the pre-invasive response, while *CsaMLO1* and *CsaMLO11* could be negative regulators in the post-invasive defense response in cucumber against *P*. *xanthii*. Although the loss-of *CsaMLO8* function confers the highest penetration resistance, *CsaMLO1* and *CsaMLO11* double mutations could be potential candidates for HR-based resistance against PM pathogen in cucumber. These results highlighted the crucial role of CRISPR/Cas9 to develop PM resistant cucumber cultivars, possessing strong pre-invasive defense with *CsaMLO8* or post-invasive with *CsaMLO1*/*CsaMLO11* mutations.

## Introduction

*Podosphaera xanthii* and *Golovinomyces cichoracearum* are the most common pathogens responsible for PM disease in cucumber (*Cucumis sativus* L.) Especially, *P. xanthii* is a serious pathogen in cucurbits, including *C. sativus* ([Zitter et al., 1996](#B45)). They are managed by fungicides in commercial agricultural production, but these chemicals affect human health and result in serious environmental problems ([Xu et al., 2017](#B42)). Also, PM pathogens could acquire resistance to fungicides with the excessive use of chemical fungicides which will be a more serious problem. Therefore, using PM-resistant cultivars is the most eco-friendly and durable management method for these pathogens. However, PM resistance is complex and polygenic and involves diverse plant defense mechanisms and signaling pathways ([Nie et al., 2015](#B30)). Generating PM-resistant cultivars with introgressed resistance (*R*) genes from wild form of cultivars is challenging for the breeders. Another concern is that the pathogen could overcome the resistance due to the mutations in avirulence genes even if the PM resistant cultivars are generated using long-term and tiresome backcrossing and inbreeding procedures.

Several susceptibility (*S*) genes were identified as an alternative for *R-*mediated resistance in the last decades, and it was supposed that the loss of *S*-gene function with controlled or naturally occurring mutations could confer durable and broad-spectrum resistance against various diseases in plants ([de Almeida Engler et al., 2005](#B14)). The proteins encoded by *S*-genes cause host recognition by the pathogen, defense suppression, and facilitate penetration ([van Schie and Takken, 2014](#B38)). These proteins are critical for disease caused by biotrophic pathogens such as PM and downy mildew because their vitality is directly associated with host interactions ([Eckardt, 2002](#B16)). *MLO*s are the most known *S*-genes responsible for PM-susceptibility in various plants. Barley *mlo* is one of the first identified genes associated with PM, and the loss of *mlo* function still confers the broad-spectrum and durable resistance against *Blumeria graminis* f.sp *hordei* (*Bgh*). Also, various research groups have reported *mlo*-mediated resistance in other plants such as *Arabidopsis thaliana* ([Consonni et al., 2006](#B13)), *Triticum aestivum* ([Konishi et al., 2010](#B25)), *Solanum lycopersicum* ([Bai et al., 2008](#B6)), *Pisum sativum* ([Humphry et al., 2011](#B20)), and *Cucumis sativus* ([Berg et al., 2017](#B7)) with phylogenetic analyses, and they revealed the fact that natural or controlled mutations on these homologs decrease PM susceptibility or confer higher resistance against PM ([Jiwan et al., 2013](#B22); [Zhou et al., 2013](#B44); [Acevedo-Garcia et al., 2014](#B1)).

*mlo*-based resistance contains different pathways and is associated with other defense mechanisms like *R-*gene-mediated PM resistance in various plants ([Xu et al., 2019](#B41)). For instance, papilla formation is a robust response against PM pathogens and is controlled by PENETRATION (PEN) and SNAREs VESICLE-ASSOCIATED MEM-BRANE PROTEINs (VAMPs) in *A. thaliana* ([Assaad et al., 2004](#B5); [Kwon et al., 2008](#B28); [Böhlenius et al., 2010](#B9)). The papilla formation is based on the accumulation of defense-related elements, such as cell-wall proteins, at the pathogen attack site ([Schmelzer, 2002](#B32); [Hückelhoven and Panstruga, 2011](#B18)). This mechanism confers the resistance not only to PM but also to the avirulent/virulent fungi at the Microbe/Pathogen-Associated Molecular Pattern (M/PAMP) Triggered Immunity (PTI) stage, which is the host’s first layer defense response ([De Wit et al., 2009](#B15)). Membrane vesicles, reactive oxygen species (ROS), and phenolic compounds contribute to host cell wall reinforcement and prevent invasion ([Kuhn et al., 2016](#B26)). Callose is another significant compound for this pre-invasive response in plants ([Ellinger and Voigt, 2014](#B17)).

Hypersensitive reaction (HR) is another robust defense response against PM pathogens and occurs in Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI) as a known second layer defense response ([Jones and Dangl, 2006](#B23)). The intercellular branching of the PM pathogens is restricted by Programmed Cell Death (PCD) with recognition of the pathogen by host receptor proteins such as receptor-like kinase (RLK) and leucine rich repeat receptor like kinases (LRK). Phytohormones and ROS also contribute to the regulation of these defense responses to restrict pathogen growth ([Micali et al., 2008](#B29); [Takken and Goverse, 2012](#B34); [Cao et al., 2014](#B10); [Serrano et al., 2014](#B33)).

*R*-gene-mediated PM resistance is multilayer like other pathogen resistance in the host. Different signaling pathways and proteins contribute to the regulation of defense response in plants ([Kuhn et al., 2016](#B26)). But how can we classify *mlo*-mediated resistance against PM pathogens? Recently, researchers classified the *mlo*-mediated resistance as a part of the non-host resistance (NHR) in *A. thaliana*. Because PEN proteins, the major compounds of NHR in *A. thaliana* against PM pathogens, are necessary for the *AtMLO2*-mediated resistance ([Humphry et al., 2006](#B19); [Appiano et al., 2015](#B4)). Although *mlo*-mediated resistance is associated with NHR for this reason, the defense mechanism is still in ambiguity, particularly in cucumber.

*C. sativus* has thirteen *CsaMLO* genes, and three of these genes (*CsaMLO1, CsaMLO8, and CsaMLO11*) were classified in Clade V associated with PM-susceptibility in dicots. It has been reported that when *CsaMLO1* and *CsaMLO11* are minor genes for PM susceptibility, *CsaMLO8* could be a major gene associated with PM susceptibility in cucumber. Also, till date there is no report on the naturally occurring mutation on *CsaMLO1* or *CsaMLO11* ([Nie et al., 2015](#B30); [Berg et al., 2015](#B8)). Although several research groups have focused on understanding *CsaMLO11/CsaML*O11 or *CsaMLO8* mediated resistance in cucumber, the defense mechanism is still unclear. Moreover, CRISPR/Cas9 for knock-out *CsaMLO1, CsaMLO8*, and *CsaMLO11* was suggested to determine *mlo-*mediated defense mechanism in cucumber ([Berg et al., 2017](#B7)).

We have previously determined the PM defense mechanism in cucumber using RT-PCR and found that callose-dependent cell-wall reinforcement was a more robust response than HR in our genotypes ([Tek and Calis, 2022](#B35)). Therefore, we presume that the major PM-susceptibility gene *CsaMLO8* could be responsible for regulating cell wall modifications negatively at pre-invasion stage. Also, we want to emphasize on the effects of the loss of function for Clade V genes in cucumber, *CsaMLO1*, and *CsaMLO11*, in defense response. Because the loss-of *CsaMLO* function is effect is still not clear in cucumber even if there are single *CsaMLO* mutant studies reported. Therefore, we used various types of loss-of *CsaMLO* function mutants such as triple and double mutants to determine *mlo*-mediated defense mechanism in cucumber. The study was carried out to reveal contribution of the *CsaMLO1, CsaMLO8*, and *CsaMLO11* mutations for pre or post invasive responses against *P*. *xanthii*. Loss of function cucumber mutants were developed to investigate defense mechanism such as penetration resistance at early stage of the infection or hypersensitive reaction in plants by using CRISPR/Cas9. Further, ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) accumulation such as H2O2 (Hydrogen Peroxide) associated with plant defense system against different plant pathogens was investigated to classify defense response which linked Clade V *CsaMLO* mutations in cucumber.

## Material and methods

### gRNAs selection and vector transformation

Three gRNAs have been selected for the *CsaMLO1, CsaMLO8*, and *CsaMLO11* by using CRISPR-GE ([Xie et al., 2017](#B40)) for Cas9 recognition in cucumber. The gRNAs were chosen from the third exon of *CsaMLO1* (Cucsa.207280) and the first exon of *CsaMLO8* (Cucsa.207280) and *CsaMLO11* (Cucsa.190600) sequences from Phytozome v12 ([Figure 1A](#f1)). pDIRECT\_23:CmYLCV\_gRNA1\_gRNA2\_gRNA3 plasmid ([Figure 1B](#f1)) was assembled from the described Golden Gate Assembly ([Čermák et al., 2017](#B11)). CmYLCV, CSY\_gRNA, REP\_gRNA, and csy\_term oligonucleotides ([Supplementary Table 1](#h10)) were used in PCR to amplify gRNAs and gRNA scaffolds for each gRNAs. High Fidelity Phusion Taq (Thermo) polymerase was used in reaction mixture according to the manufacturer protocol. PCR was conducted in; pre-denaturation at 98°C for 30 secs, 30 cycle; denaturation at 98°C for 10 secs, annealing at 65 °C for 30 secs, elongation at 72 °C for 15 secs, and final elongation at 72°C for 5 mins conditions to amplify gRNA:gRNA\_scaffold and pDIRECT\_23C was used as a template. 50 ng pDIRECT\_23C ([Supplementary Figure 1](#h10)), 0.5 µl of diluted (ten times) PCR products, 0.5 µl *SapI*, 0.5 µl *Esp3I*, 1 µl T7 DNA ligase, 10 µl 2x T7 DNA ligase buffer, and 7 µl ddH2O were used in Golden Gate reactions. The ligation was completed in 10 cycles at 37°C for 5 minutes and 25°C for 10 minutes. *Pme*I and *Pml*I restriction enzymes were used to confirm the plasmid and transferred to *E. coli* DH5α ([Supplementary Figure 2](#h10)). The transformation plasmid was isolated from DH5α after validation with kanamycin (50mg/L) and ccdB selection. Transformation plasmid was transferred to electrocompetent *Agrobacterium tumefaciens* EHA105 by electroporation. PC primers (annealing at 57°C) were used in colony PCR to confirm transformation of EHA105.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1081506/fpls-13-1081506-HTML/image_m/fpls-13-1081506-g001.jpg)

**Figure 1** Specific gRNAs chosen from the coding site of Clade V *CsaMLO* (CDS) in cucumber and pDIRECT\_23C plasmid. **(A)** gRNA1 for *CsaMLO1*, gRNA2 for *CsaMLO8*, and gRNA3 for *CsaMLO11* were selected by using CRISPR-GE. **(B)** Selected gRNAs were amplified with PCR and cloned into pDIRECT\_23C’s. Cestrum yellow leaf curling virus promoter (CmYLCV) was used for gRNAs expression and bialaphos phosphinothricin resistance (BlpR) was used to transgenic plant selection. Also, pDIRECT\_23C contains Cauliflower Mosaic Virus 35S promoter (CaMV35) for Cas9.

### Transformation and plant regeneration

Cotyledons of PM-susceptible cucumber line ADR27 were used as explants for *A. tumefaciens* EHA105-mediated transformation. The transformation and regeneration procedure were conducted as previously described ([Zhang et al., 2017](#B43)), with slight modifications. ADR27 inbred line’s seeds were kept in tap water for 2 hours and the seed coats were removed. Seed surfaces were sterilized using 75% ethanol for 30 seconds. Further, commercial bleach (3% sodium hypochlorite solution) was used also for sterilization for 15 minutes after the ethanol was drained out. After rinsing three times with deionized water and drying on sterile filter paper aseptically under laminar airflow cabinet, the seeds were placed in a petri dish containing plant germination medium (Murashige Skoog with 3% sucrose). Meanwhile, EHA-105 was incubated in Luria-Bertani (LB) broth containing 100 mg l-1 rifampicin and 50 mg l-1 kanamycin for 16-24 hours at 28°C and at 200 rpm. Bacterial cells were transferred to 50 ml of LB broth and incubated until the OD at 600 nm reached 0.6-0.8. After centrifugation at 4000 g for 10 minutes, the bacterial pellet was diluted in MS liquid to OD600 = 0.25. Cotyledon explants were taken from a germination medium using sterile forceps and were prepared for transformation using a sterile scalpel. Prepared explants were transferred to the EHA105-containing liquid MS, incubated for 15 minutes for agroinfiltration, and placed in a co-cultivation Medium (MS with 3% sucrose, 2 mg ml-1 BAP, 2 mg ml-1 ABA, and 200 μM acetosyringone) at 28°C for two days in the dark keeping the abaxial surface in contact with the culture medium. The explants were then transferred onto a shooting medium (MS with 3% sucrose, 2 mg ml-1 BAP, 2 mg ml-1 ABA, 150 mg l-1 Timentin) containing 1 mg ml-1 glufosinate-ammonium to select transformant plants. After 2-3 weeks of incubation at 25°C under 16-hour photoperiod, the resulting shoots were transferred to glass jars containing a rooting medium (MS with 3% sucrose, 150 mg l-1 timentin and 1 mg ml-1 glufosinate-ammonium). After 2-4 weeks, the rooted plants were transferred to pots containing wet compost, covered with a zip bag, and grown for 6-8 weeks at 25°C/18°C and 16 hours photoperiod.

### Development of T1 plants

DNA was isolated from T0 plants using the GeneJET Genomic DNA Purification Kit, and transformation was confirmed by using Cas9 primers ([Supplementary Table 1](#h10)). After acclimatization, 0.1 M silver nitrate was sprayed on newly formed shoots to form male flowers for selfing. The transgenic T0 ADR27 male flowers, which opened on the same day, were crossed with female flowers. After the fruits ripened and matured, the T1 seeds were harvested, washed with 70% ethanol, dried, and stored at 4°C.

### Analysis of mutations in T1 plants

The T1 seeds were germinated in pots containing wet compost. Genomic DNA was extracted from the T1 cotyledons using GeneJET Genomic DNA Purification Kit as per manufacturer’s instructions (Thermo Fisher, Scientific, USA). Meanwhile, WT-specific forward primers were designed from downstream and upstream of the PAM sequence in for *CsaMLO1, CsaMLO8*, and *CsaMLO11*. Reverse primers were designed from GC-rich regions of these genes with high-annealing temperatures to increase specificity. The method is previously described as an “annealing at critical temperature PCR” (ACT-PCR) by [Wang and Wang, 2019](#B39). Critical temperatures were determined with the gradient-PCR (60°C to 72°C) by using DNA isolated from WT-ADR27. PCR was carried out under the following conditions: initial denaturation at 94°C for 2 minutes, denaturation at 94°C for 30 seconds, annealing at 68°C (for *CsaMLO1* and *CsaMLO11*) and 65°C (for *CsaMLO8*) for 30 seconds, elongation at 72°C for 45 seconds; 30 cycle and final elongation at 72°C for 5 minutes to detect homozygous mutant. The PCR products were visualized after the agarose gel (1.5%) electrophoresis. The DNA from homozygous mutant plants that did not show amplification in the PCR using these primers could be attributed to the deletion or insertion mutation on the PAM sequence.

### Determination of the *CsaMLO* mutation types

*CsaMLO1, CsaMLO8*, and *CsaMLO11* gRNA targets of selected mutant plants accordingly ACT-PCR results were amplified with PCR by using the CsaMLO-Seq primers ([Supplementary Table 1](#h10)). PCR were carried out; pre-denutaration at 94°C for 5 mins; denaturation at 94°C for 1 min, annealing at 58°C for 45 secs, elongation at 72°C for 45 secs and final elongation at 72°C for 5 mins. The products were then run on 1.5% agarose gel using gel electrophoresis for confirmation. Finally, the amplicon was purified and sequenced using the Sanger method by Macrogen (Madrid, Spain). The T1 plants and WT ADR27 gRNA target sequences were evaluated with alignment of WT and mutant *CsaMLO* genes. Mutations were detected for each sequenced plant and are presented in [Figure 2](#f2). The introns sequences were cleaned with reference CoDing Sequence (CDS) alignment. Cucsa.207280, Cucsa.308270, and Cucsa.190600 Phytozome reference sequences were used for *CsaMLO1*, *CsaMLO8*, and *CsaMLO11* respectively. CDSs were converted to amino acid sequence in GeniousPrime (2021.2.2v) and protein sequences of mutant were aligned with WT-ADR27. Open Reading Frame (ORF) shifts and stop codon formations were determined according to the alignment result.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1081506/fpls-13-1081506-HTML/image_m/fpls-13-1081506-g002.jpg)

**Figure 2** Nucleotide sequence alignment of *CsaMLO*s. Clade V *CsaMLOs* of WT-ADR27 were aligned with the reference sequence to detect any genotypic differentiation, and the underlined nucleotides on the alignment shows the PCR primers for detection of homozygous mutants; **(A)** the sequence results indicated that 4-11 has deletion mutations on *CsaMLO8* (2 bp) and *CsaMLO11* (4 bp) even if there is no mutation on the *CsaMLO*1. Although 4-14 is triple mutant (2 bp del at *CsaMLO*8 and 4 bp del at *CsaMLO11*), there is substitution mutation on *CsaMLO*1 with 2 nucleotides change as “AA” rather than “GC” on the Cas9 cleavage site. 4-15 and 2-18 were determined as *CsaMLO1*del with 11 bp and 8 bp deletions as a result of NHEJ. Also 8 bp deletions were detected on 4-21’s *CsaMLO1* and *CsaMLO11*; **(B)** The PCR products were observed to be non-mutant genotype in terms of *CsaMLO*. However, there was no amplification in PCR results of mutant, because of homozygous deletion or substitution on the primer binding sites.

### Inoculation and evaluation of reactions against PM

*P. xanthii* conidiospores were harvested from the PM infected plants with a brush and suspended in distilled water to give 2x105 spore ml-1 and sprayed three times onto T1 mutant and susceptible WT ADR27 cucumber leaves. Ten days after the inoculation, leaf samples were taken from the PM inoculated WT and mutant plants, boiled in 96% ethanol to remove the chlorophyll, and then stained with trypan blue (250 µg ml-1). The development of conidiophores and conidiospores was examined under microscope (Leica DM500). Conidiospores were counted on five leaves for each plant within 100 spores at a 0.02 mm2 area for detection of spore germination and germination/penetration rates were compared between susceptible WT-ADR27 and mutants. Determination of ROS accumulation was carried out by staining leaves with DAB combined with Trypan Blue ([Thordal-Christensen et al., 1997](#B37)). For this, the PM-inoculated leaves were incubated for 6 hours in DAB (1%) solution and boiled in 96% ethanol. Leaves were evaluated and scored using a modified DR (Disease Reaction) scale where 0 indicates no fungal growth and 4 indicates dense fungal growth ([Adam and Somerville, 1996](#B2)).

## Results

### Plant regeneration and development of T1 mutants

After germination of ADR27 seeds, cotyledons were removed and used as explants for transformation using *A. tumefaciens* EHA 105 and were then transferred to co-cultivation medium. The explants were shifted to an MS medium containing 1 mg ml-1 glufosinate-ammonium for selection of transformants. The integration of T-DNA into plant genome was confirmed by PCR using Cas9 specific primers and 46 transgenic plants were regenerated at T0. Regeneration steps and T0 plants were given in [Supplementary Figure 3](#h10). 96 T1 plants were also examined using ACT-PCR, and the result revealed that the mutation rates of the *CsaMLO* genes observed in the T1 generation were 25%, 2.08% and 19.79% for *CsaMLO1*, *CsaMLO8*, and *CsaMLO11*, respectively ([Supplementary Table 2](#h10)).

### In/dels in mutants

*CsaMLO1* did not amplify in PCR that was carried out with isolated DNA samples from 4-14, 4-15, 4-21, and 2-18 lines. *CsaMLO8* was amplified when the T1 plants, excluding 4-11 and 4-14 were used in PCR. Also, *CsaMLO11* did not amplify in 4-11, 4-14, and 4-21. The sequences of the gRNA’s target of these lines revealed that these lines have deletion and substitution mutations on the Cas9 cleavage site. 4-14 have 2 nucleotide substitution mutations on the upstream of the PAM motif. The deletion mutations were detected in the 4-15, 4-21, and 2-18 cucumber lines. The *CsaMLO1* deletion mutations in mutant lines were relatively larger than the *CsaMLO8* deletions. The small deletions (2 bp) are seen upstream of the PAM in the *CsaMLO8* of 4-11 and 4-14 T1 plants. Deletions were also detected in the *CsaMLO11* of 4-11, 4-14, and 4-21 T1 plants. The *CsaMLO11* deletions are 4 and 8 bp, and the location of the deletions differed from the PAM motif of *CsaMLO1*. The position of deletions ranged from downstream of the PAM to upstream in *CsaMLO1*, but the deletion was detected only upstream of the PAM in *CsaMLO11* ([Figure 2](#f2)).

### Early stop codon formation and amino acid substitution

Most of the mutations occurred in different mutant plants caused early stop codon formation at the amino acid sequence except that in 4-14 mutant plant, two nucleotide mutations (GC to AA) in *CsaMLO*1 lead to alanine to asparagine change at the 137th amino acid as a result NHEJ (non-homologous end joining) after the Cas9 cleavage. The alanine was changed asparagine at the 137th amino acid of *CsaMLO1* as a result NHEJ (non-homologous end joining) after the Cas9 cleavage. The stop codon formation was detected at the 140th amino acid of 4-21 and 2-18’s *CsaMLO1* caused by 8 bp deletion, while the stop codon was also detected on 139th amino acid of 4-15’s *CsaMLO1* caused by 11 bp deletion. The ORF shifts were detected on the *CsaMLO8* mutants 4-11 and 4-14 at Cas9 cleavage site and stop codon formation was detected at the 33rd amino acid. ORF shift and stop codons also detected in *CsaMLO11* as a result of NHEJ. The stop codons were at the 26th amino acid of 4-11 and 4-14 *CsaMLO11*, while stop codon formation appeared at 57th amino acid of 4-21’s *CsaMLO11*. 4-11 (*CsaMLO8/11*del), 4-14 (*CsaMLO1*A137N/*CsaMLO*8/11del), 4-15 (*CsaMLO1*del), 4-21 (*CsaMLO1*/11del), and 2-18 (*CsaMLO1*del) were determined as loss of function mutant ([Figure 3](#f3)).

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1081506/fpls-13-1081506-HTML/image_m/fpls-13-1081506-g003.jpg)

**Figure 3** Amino acid alignment using sequences of mutants and WT plants. **(A)** When the deletions on the Cas9 cleavage (red arrow) cause stop codon formation on the 4-15’s *CsaMLO1* 139th amino acid; the stop codon formation was detected on 2-18 and 4-21 *CsaMLO1’s* 140th amino acid (3rd exon). The Alanine **(A)** was altered with Asparagine (N) at the 137th amino acid as a result of the NHEJ caused by Cas9 cleavage at the gRNA1 target. **(B)** 2 bp deletions cause the unexpected stop codon formation on the 33rd amino acid of *CsaMLO8* in 4-11 and 4-14. The target site of gRNA2 was on the first exon for *CsaMLO8* and the changed amino acid structures are at the Cas9 cleavage (10th aa) site for this gene. The early stop codon formation caused the loss-of *CsaMLO8* function in 4-11 and 4-14. **(C)** The mutations seem to be responsible for the stop codon formation in *CsaMLO*11 and stop codon’s positions of 4-11 and 4-14’s *CsaMLO11* closer to that gRNA3 than 4-15 and 2-18 *CsaMLO11*. However, ORF shifts are initiated at gRNA3 position in whole *CsaMLO11*del.

### Evaluation of mutants for *P. xanthii* resistance

The *P. xanthii* was inoculated on 4-11 (*CsaMLO8*/11del), 4-14 (*CsaMLO1*A137N/*CsaMLO*8/11del), 4-15 (*CsaMLO1*del), 4-21 (*CsaMLO1*/11del), 2-18 (*CsaMLO1*del), and WT cucumber plants after the determination of the mutation and mutation types. After ten days of inoculation, inoculated five leaves were examined closely for inoculated WT and mutant plants to determine DR scores ([Table 1](#T1)). Further, leaves were also harvested from the WT and mutant plants, chlorophyll was removed, and staining was done with Trypan Blue. Typical PM symptoms were apparent on WT ADR27 leaves. 4-15 and 2-18 mutants showed fewer symptoms on the inoculated leaves. The PM symptoms on 4-21 and 4-11 plants were less severe than in *CsaMLO1*del plants. Furthermore, there were no symptoms on the 4-14 plant. The microscopic observation results confirmed that the conidiospores had germinated massively and developed many new spores on the WT ADR27 plant. However, no new conidiophores or conidiospores were observed on the inoculated *CsaMLO8*/11del and *CsaMLO1*A137N/*CsamMLO8*/11del ([Figure 4](#f4)). Low spore germination and limited mycelial growth was observed on the 4-11 and 4-14 plants.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1081506/fpls-13-1081506-HTML/image_m/fpls-13-1081506-t001.jpg)

**Table 1** DR scores at 10 dpi on different PM-inoculated leaves.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1081506/fpls-13-1081506-HTML/image_m/fpls-13-1081506-g004.jpg)

**Figure 4** Evaluation of the DR scores against PM and *P. xanthi* penetration/germination rates at ten-day post-inoculation. **(A)** DR scores of wild-type ADR27 and mutant plants were generated means of the inoculated five leaves for each genotypes at 10 dpi ([Table 1](#T1)) and same colors indicated that there is no difference for DR scores statistically according to standard deviation bar. The lowest DR scores were observed in 4-11 (*CsaMLO8*/11del) and 4-14 (*CsaMLO1*A137N/8/11del). The DR scores were statistically similar for 4-15 and 2-18 plants having *CsaMLO1* mutation. 4-21 was determined as *CsaMLO1*/11 del mutant and the DR score was lower than *CsaMLO1*del even if higher DR score than 4-11 and 4-14. **(B)** The strong penetration resistance is defined in 4-11 and 4-14 plants having loss of *CsaMLO8* function. Also, *P. xanthii* germination rate was lowest in these mutants. 4-15 and 2-18 (*CsaMLO1*del) penetration and germination rates were similar with the *CsaMLO1*/11del. Percentages of the germination and penetration rates were created according to average of germinated and penetrated spores’ numbers from each genotype’s five leaves.

### ROS accumulation on *CsaMLO8*/11del and *CsaMLO1*A137N/CsaMLO8/11del

Trypan blue staining revealed massive mesophyll cell death on ADR27 associated with PM infection. However, there were restricted death cells in 4-11 and 4-14 plants ([Figure 5](#f5)). The DAB and Trypan Blue combined staining also shows ROS accumulation on ADR27 and mutant plants. ROS accumulation was observed near the *P. xanthii* mycelium growth area of the leaves in both mutant and WT plants. Notably, on 4-14 mesophyll cells, the pathogen conidiospores that were unable to germinate were quite visible, even though there was no ROS accumulation and cell death. Combined staining also revealed that the mycelial growth was on wild-type ADR27 leaves, and ROS was accumulated in this growth area. The maximum ROS accumulation and *P. xanthii* development were on the WT ADR27 when compared with 4-11 and 4-14 ([Figure 6](#f6)).

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1081506/fpls-13-1081506-HTML/image_m/fpls-13-1081506-g005.jpg)

**Figure 5** Powdery mildew symptoms and visualization of *P. xanthii* spores on WT/mutant leaves using Trypan Blue. **(A)** Powdery mildew symptoms on leaf surfaces of WT-ADR27 and *CsaMLO* mutants at 10 dpi. Typical symptoms could be seen on the WT leaves as a result massive propagation caused by *P. xanthii* after ten-days post inoculation. Restricted symptoms were detected on *P. xanthii* inoculated leaves of 4-15, 4-21, and 2-18 plants. Moreover, the hypersensitive response associated

enriched_points.expanded_content: CRISPR technology has successfully enhanced cucumber's resistance to powdery mildew by targeting and mutating susceptibility genes, creating cultivars with durable and broad-spectrum fungal resistance.
Implementing CRISPR-edited fungal-resistant cucumbers can significantly reduce reliance on fungicides, leading to lower production costs, enhanced yields, and improved market competitiveness.

Future research should focus on expanding CRISPR applications in cucumbers, exploring resistance mechanisms against emerging pathogens, and ensuring regulatory compliance for sustainable agricultural practices.
